title: Understanding the bee year in the UK
subtitle: A month-by-month guide to colony development and beekeeper tasks.
excerpt: A honeybee colony does not behave the same way in April as it does in October. The colony's needs, risks, and activities change with the season. This guide walks through the year month by month, covering what the bees are doing and what you should be doing at each point.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Annual Beekeeping Calendar (2024). BBKA Good Practice Guide, seasonal management chapter. Ted Hooper, Guide to Bees and Honey (1976), seasonal overview. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The tight ball of bees that forms inside the hive when temperatures drop below about 10°C. The cluster generates heat by vibrating their flight muscles, consuming honey stores to fuel the process. The queen stays at the warmest centre of the cluster.

The rapid increase in colony population from February to May as the queen increases her laying rate and the first winter workers are replaced by the new spring generation. A colony can grow from 5,000 bees in January to 50,000 bees by May.

A period with little or no nectar coming into the hive. Colonies under dearth conditions may turn aggressive, attempt robbing of weaker hives, and consume stores. Dearth can occur in June between spring and summer flows, in August after heather, or any time the weather prevents flying.

content.text: The bee year follows temperature, day length, and flower availability. It does not match the human calendar. The broad phases are: winter cluster (November to February),

and swarm preparation (March to June), peak colony and honey production (June to August), colony contraction and winter preparation (August to October), and winter (November to February again). A beekeeper works alongside these phases, not against them.

once temperatures drop below 10°C. Brood rearing stops or nearly stops. The queen lays very few eggs, if any. The bees consume stored honey to generate heat. Your job is minimal: a fortnightly heft to check stores, a check that the entrance is not blocked, and a December or January oxalic acid treatment for varroa while the colony is broodless. Do not open the hive.

On the first warm days above 10°C in late February, bees make short cleansing flights. The queen begins to increase her laying rate in response to longer days. Early pollen from snowdrops, crocus, and willow starts coming in. This is the most dangerous time of year for starvation: the colony is growing fast but spring forage has barely started. Heft the hive more often. If it feels light, add fondant.

The colony grows rapidly once fruit trees and willow flower. By mid-April a strong colony may have six to eight frames of brood. Make the first proper inspection of the year on a calm day above 15°C. Look for: laying pattern, stores, signs of disease, and the first queen cups. Add a super when seven or more frames are covered with bees. Start weekly inspections now.

This is the busiest and most demanding period of the beekeeper year. The colony is at or near peak population. Swarm pressure is highest. Inspect every seven days without fail. Look for queen cells on every frame every time. Have a nucleus box and spare equipment ready for splits. The spring honey flow from hawthorn and fruit blossom is the richest nectar of the year; a colony that swarms now loses half its foragers at the peak of the flow.

attrs.title: The June gap
attrs.body: In many parts of the UK, June brings a sudden drop in incoming nectar once spring blossom has finished and before white clover and summer plants open. A large colony with lots of mouths to feed can eat through stores fast. Check hive weight or look for capped stores on the outer frames during June inspections. Feed if necessary.

content.text: White clover, lavender, and late summer wildflowers support the main honey flow. Keep supers on and check weekly for capped frames. Harvest when frames are at least 75 percent capped. Remove supers once the flow ends, usually mid-August. By late August, start preparing for autumn.

can set in suddenly in late July or August if the weather turns hot and dry. Colonies under dearth conditions turn defensive and may attempt to rob neighbouring hives. Close entrances down to a few bee widths. Do not open feeders or leave honey or syrup exposed outdoors.

Carry out the full autumn assessment in late August or early September. Treat for varroa. Feed heavy syrup if stores are short. Fit the mouse guard in October. Remove any remaining supers. Make the combination decision for weak colonies. Once the daytime temperature drops reliably below 15°C and the bees are clustering at the entrance in the evening, the season is over.
